clean_output:
article_id: art-2026-03-03-008
summary: The package shows a mixed record where context files can worsen task success and raise execution burden, yet help under different measurement or documentation conditions.
review_confidence: high
contradictions:
  - contradiction_text: LLM-generated context files reduced task success in most ETH Zurich settings, but a separate JAWs 2026 study associated AGENTS.md with faster completion and lower token output.
    related_claim_texts:
      - The ETH Zurich study found that LLM-generated context files reduced coding-agent task success in most tested settings rather than improving it.
      - In the ETH Zurich study, adding context files increased execution burden even when performance gains were absent or small.
    related_fact_refs:
      - fact-004
      - fact-006
      - fact-007
      - fact-008
      - fact-012
      - fact-013
    related_evidence_ids:
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-06
      - evidence-07
      - evidence-08
      - evidence-12
      - evidence-13
    disposition: bounded
    disclosure: Keep visible that the studies measure different outcomes and designs; ETH Zurich reports weaker task success and higher burden, while JAWs reports better speed and token efficiency, so the record does not support a simple overall verdict.
    confidence: high
  - contradiction_text: LLM-generated context files underperformed in 5 of 8 ETH Zurich settings, but improved performance when existing repository documentation was removed.
    related_claim_texts:
      - The ETH Zurich study found that LLM-generated context files reduced coding-agent task success in most tested settings rather than improving it.
      - The study supports treating context files mainly as execution constraints whose usefulness depends on how minimally and appropriately they are written.
    related_fact_refs:
      - fact-004
      - fact-014
    related_evidence_ids:
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-14
    disposition: bounded
    disclosure: Keep visible that context-file effects depend on documentation conditions; generated files appear harmful in most tested settings but can help when they fill a missing documentation gap.
